coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots originating from unripe peach for fluorescent bioimaging and electrocatalytic oxygen reduction reaction
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Atchudan, Raji
Edison, Thomas Nesakumar Jebakumar Immanuel
Lee, Yong Rok
coredata.dc:description: This paper reports the robust hydrothermal synthesis of nitrogen doped carbon dots (N-CDs) using the unripe fruit of Prunus persica (peach) as the carbon precursor and aqueous ammonia as the nitrogen source. The optical properties of synthesized N-CDs were characterized by ultraviolet visible (UV–Vis) and fluorescence spectroscopy techniques. The synthesized N-CDs were emitted blue light when excitated with a portable UV lamp. The materials with the optical properties were characterized further by high resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), Raman, Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The mean size of the N-CDs was approximately 8nm, as calculated from the HRTEM image. The d-spacing of N-CDs, calculated using Bragg law, was approximately 0.21nm, which was consistent with the interlayer distance calculated from the HRTEM image. FT-IR spectroscopy and XPS revealed the presence of the phytoconstituents functionalities of peach fruit over the N-CDs surface and a high level of nitrogen doping on carbon dots (CDs) was confirmed by XPS studies. These results suggest that the unripe fruit extract of peach is an ideal candidate for the preparation of N-CDs. The resulting N-CDs showed excellent optical properties in water. The synthesized N-CDs exhibited a high fluorescence quantum yield and low cytotoxicity, and can be used as fluorescence imaging probes. In addition, the N-CDs were catalytically activite towards the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR). The N-CDs exhibited good catalytic activity in an alkaline medium (0.1M KOH) with a remarkable ORR of approximately 0.72V vs reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE), and O2 reduction follows mainly a 2 electron pathway by being reduced to hydrogen peroxide. The 2-electron reduction pathway is used in industry for H2O2 production.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Peach
Hydrothermal-carbonization
Carbon dot
Fluorescence
Bioimaging
Oxygen reduction reaction

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of extract of peach and N-CDs
Electrocatalytic oxygen reduction reaction of N-CDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Ministry of Education, Science and Technology
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Education, Science and Technology
Ministry of Science, ICT and Future Planning

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supported by the Nano Material Technology Development Program of the Korean National Research Foundation (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Education, Science and Technology ( 2012M3A7B4049675 ). This work was also supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) grant funded by the Korea government (MSIP) ( NRF-2014R1A2A1A11052391 ) and Priority Research Centers Program ( 2014R1A6A1031189 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration for the preparation of N-CDs.

TEM images (inset: HRTEM images of single N-CD (a)), particle size distribution (b), HAADF (c) and corresponding mapping images (d) carbon, (e) nitrogen, (f) oxygen, (g) EDAX and (h) AFM topography image of resulting N-CDs.
Wide-angle XRD pattern (a) and Raman spectrum (b) of resulted N-CDs; FT-IR spectra of (c) unripe fruit extract of peach and (d) the resulting N-CDs (Inset is proposed structure of synthesized N-CDs).
XPS spectra of (a) survey scan, (b) carbon, (c) nitrogen and (d) oxygen for the resulting N-CDs.
(a) UV–Vis spectrum and (b) excitation dependent fluorescence spectra of resulting N-CDs.

(a) The CV curves of ORR in N2 and O2 saturated 0.1M KOH electrolyte at the scan rate of 10mVs−1, (b) RDE polarization curve at 1600rpm under N2 and O2 saturation (inset: magnified view of potential 0.7–1V vs RHE), (c) RDE polarization curves in O2 saturated 0.1M KOH electrolyte with different rotating speeds at a scan rate of 10mVs−1 and (d) The K–L plots at different electrode potentials of N-CDs.

(a) Cell viability by CCK-8 assay of MDA-MB-231 cells incubated with N-CDs for 4h; Fluorescent microscopy images of MDA-MB-231 cells after the cellular uptake of N-CDs (20μgmL−1) for 4h. The ultra violet excitation and the emission were obtained by fluorescent microscopy (b) with N-CDs in bright field, (c) with N-CDs in fluorescence, (d) without N-CDs in bright field (e) without N-CDs in fluorescence.
Comparison of optical characteristics, size, quantum yield and application of the CDs derived from different eco-friendly carbon precursor with the reported methods.

row.entry: Fe(III) ions detection and cell imaging
Fe(III) ions detection and cell imaging

head.ce:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots originating from unripe peach for fluorescent bioimaging and electrocatalytic oxygen reduction reaction

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, Yeungnam University, Gyeongsan 38541, Republic of Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This paper reports the robust hydrothermal synthesis of N-CDs using the unripe fruit of peach as the carbon precursor and aqueous ammonia as the nitrogen source. The synthesized N-CDs exhibits a good catalytic activity in an alkaline medium (0.1M KOH) with remarkable ORR and highly biocompatible thus it can be used as fluorescence imaging probes.

This paper reports the robust hydrothermal synthesis of nitrogen doped carbon dots (N-CDs) using the unripe fruit of Prunus persica (peach) as the carbon precursor and aqueous ammonia as the nitrogen source. The optical properties of synthesized N-CDs were characterized by ultraviolet visible (UV–Vis) and fluorescence spectroscopy techniques. The synthesized N-CDs were emitted blue light when excitated with a portable UV lamp. The materials with the optical properties were characterized further by high resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), Raman, Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The mean size of the N-CDs was approximately 8nm, as calculated from the HRTEM image. The d-spacing of N-CDs, calculated using Bragg law, was approximately 0.21nm, which was consistent with the interlayer distance calculated from the HRTEM image. FT-IR spectroscopy and XPS revealed the presence of the phytoconstituents functionalities of peach fruit over the N-CDs surface and a high level of nitrogen doping on carbon dots (CDs) was confirmed by XPS studies. These results suggest that the unripe fruit extract of peach is an ideal candidate for the preparation of N-CDs. The resulting N-CDs showed excellent optical properties in water. The synthesized N-CDs exhibited a high fluorescence quantum yield and low cytotoxicity, and can be used as fluorescence imaging probes. In addition, the N-CDs were catalytically activite towards the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR). The N-CDs exhibited good catalytic activity in an alkaline medium (0.1M KOH) with a remarkable ORR of approximately 0.72V vs reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE), and O2 reduction follows mainly a 2 electron pathway by being reduced to hydrogen peroxide. The 2-electron reduction pathway is used in industry for H2O2 production.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, photoluminescent nanomaterials have attracted considerable attentions owing to their attractive properties, such as optical and electronic properties. Semiconductor quantum dots (SQDs) and organic dyes have attracted much attention for their potential applications in many fields such as photoluminescence, sensors, energy and bio-imaging [1–4]. On other hand, the heavy metal containing SQDs which may lead to potential cytotoxicity in biological systems and the photostability of organic dyes is poor. This drawback can be overcome by a new fluorescent nanomaterials, photoluminescent carbon dots (CDs) as a new class of nanoparticles, which have attracted considerable attention due to exceptional advantages, such as low toxicity, high chemical stability, good biocompatibility, excellent optical performance, and low photo bleaching. Recently, nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) have great attention due to their fascinating properties and applications, one of which is the highly enhanced photoluminescence [5–14].

The most common methods for the synthesis of the CDs, include the chemical oxidation method, ultrasonic method, hydrothermal synthesis, solvothermal method, microwave method, and laser ablation method. However, it is still a problem for progress in controlling the morphology, particle size, and surface chemistry of the resultant products with high quantum yields. The green chemical routes of CDs from plant materials are simple and eco-friendly. Authors already reported the synthesis of florescent CDs using Prunus mume and were applied as a straining probe for bio-imaging application [15]. In the present study, Prunus persica (peach) was chosen as a carbon source for the synthesizing CDs due to it is easily available and affordable, and were consumed as a common food and medicinal materials. Peach belongs to family rosaceae is known for its nutritional value and therapeutic properties. The major constituents of peach fruit are carbohydrates, organic acids, minerals, and dietary fibre, which contribute to the nutritional quality of both the fresh fruits and the juice [16,17]. Peach being a potential source of bioactive compounds has been demonstrated to possess medicinal properties. Phenolic acids, flavonoids, and anthocyanin are the major sources of potential antioxidants in peach fruit. The nanomaterial synthesis and activity of them depends on the phytoconstituents present in the particular extract. The sucrose and glucose were dominant sugars, and citric acid was dominant organic acids in unripe peach fruits, this might be helpful to formation of carbon materials. The unripe fruits possess more acidic constituents (citric acid) compared to ripen fruits [18]. The glucose content nearly same for ripen as well as unripe fruits. Based on the above reasons, the unripe peach has been chosen as a carbon source for the synthesis of CDs. Recently, hydrothermal-carbonization has proved to be an eco-friendly, easy and soft chemical route for the synthesis of CDs and N-CDs in aqueous media, which produces highly efficient fluorescent probes for cell imaging in biomedical application [19–22]. The synthesized N-CDs have also been used as an electrocatalyst for the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) which is an important reaction in energy converting systems, such as fuel cells [23–26]. ORR in aqueous solutions occurs mainly by two pathways: the direct 4-electron reduction from O2 to H2O, and the 2-electron reduction from O2 to hydrogen peroxide (H2O2)·H2O2 has been one of the most important chemicals in the world and the 2-electron reduction pathway is used in industry for H2O2 production [27–29].

Herein, highly photoluminescent N-CDs with narrow size distribution were synthesized for the first time by simple hydrothermal process using the extract of unripe peach fruit and aqueous ammonia as the carbon precursor and nitrogen source, respectively. Aqueous ammonia was also used as a nitrogen dopant of the peach fruit extract. The optical properties of obtained nanoparticles were examined by using UV–Vis and fluorescence spectroscopy in water. In Addition, the fluorescent N-CDs exhibited hypotoxicity and can be a biocompatible candidate for bio-imaging in living cells. The synthesized N-CDs were used as catalyst towards ORR. To the best of our knowledge, the unripe peach is first time used for the synthesis of N-CDs and as a staining probe for fluorescence cell imaging and electrocatalyst for ORR.

The unripe peach fruit was collected from the Yeungnam University Campus, Gyeongsan, Republic of Korea. An aqueous ammonia solution (25%), quinine sulphate, isopropyl alcohol (IPA) and Nafion solution (∼5%) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich and were used as received. The MDA-MB-231 cells were procured from Korean cell line bank, Seoul, Republic of Korea. The deionized (DI) water was used throughout this study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of extract of peach and N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The unripe fruits of peach were washed thoroughly with DI water and cut into small slices without seed. The sliced unripe fruit was ground well using a mixer grinder. The grounded peach was filtered through cotton followed by Whatman filter paper (Whatman grade 40, thickness 210μm and pore size 8μm) and washed with small amount of DI water. The peach extract was collected and the aqueous ammonia was added for nitrogen doping on the CDs during the hydrothermal-carbonization process.

The N-CDs were prepared as follows: Initially, 25mL of peach extract was taken into a Teflon cup with 50mL inner volume. Additionally, 2mL of aqueous ammonia solution was added into the peach extract and the Teflon cup was slid into a stainless steel autoclave. The autoclave was sealed and placed into a hot air oven and heated to 180°C for 5h. During this process, the solution changed from pale yellow to dark brown, which indicated the formation of CDs. Then, the solution was naturally cooled to room temperature and centrifuged at 10,000rpm for 15min to remove the larger or agglomerated particles (residue). The supernatant was collected and dried using freeze-dryer for removal of water from the synthesized N-CDs. The dried solid material was stored for further use. Fig. 1 shows a schematic illustration for the preparation of N-CDs.

The synthesized N-CDs were characterized using various physico-chemical techniques, such as UV–Vis spectroscopy, fluorescence spectroscopy, X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM), atomic force microscopy (AFM) and Raman spectroscopy. The optical properties of the N-CDs were analyzed using an OPTIZEN 3220UV and Hitachi-7000 F spectrometer. The excitation wavelength was varied from lower to higher wavelengths to determine the maximum luminescence intensity for the fluorescence measurements. The slit width was fixed to 5nm and the scan speed was set to 240nm/min. The XRD patterns were obtained on a PANalytical X’Pert3 MRD diffractometer using Cu Kα radiation (λ=1.54Å, 40kV and 30mA) equipped with a liquid nitrogen cooled germanium solid-state detector. The XRD patterns of N-CDs were recorded in the range, 10–90° 2θ, and at the step interval of 0.02° with a counting time of 5s at each point. The FT-IR spectra were recorded in transmittance mode on an ATR-FTIR spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer Spectrum Two) over the wavenumber range, 400–4000cm−1 by the co-addition of 50 scans at a resolution of 16cm−1. XPS was performed using a K-Alpha (Thermo Scientific instrument). Casa XPS instrument software was used for the deconvolution of XPS. The HRTEM images of N-CDs were obtained using a FEI-Tecnai TF-20 operated at an acceleration voltage of 200kV. Samples for HRTEM were prepared by placing droplets of a suspension of the N-CDs in DI water onto a carbon-coated polymer micro grid supported on a Cu grid and dried at ambient temperature under static condition. Topography images were obtained using Atomic force microscopy (AFM) (Tapping mode-NanoscopeIIIa, Digital Instruments, Inc.). Raman spectra of the N-CDs were recorded on a thermo scientific DXR SmartRaman spectrometer with laser excitation at 532nm.

The quantum yield (QY) of the synthesized N-CDs was calculated by measuring the fluorescence intensity in an aqueous dispersion by using the following Eq. (1) [30–32]:
(1)
QY
CDs
=
QY
R
I
CDs
A
R
(
n
CDs
)
2
I
R
A
CDs
(
n
R
)
2
where QYCDs and QYR are the fluorescence quantum yield of N-CDs and reference, respectively. (Reference: Quinine sulphate, QYR =0.54), ICDs and IR are the integrated fluorescence intensity of N-CDs and reference, respectively. ACDs and AR are the UV–Vis absorption intensity of N-CDs (0.727) and reference (0.721), respectively. n is the refractive index (1) of the solvent for both N-CDs and the reference.

The MDA-MB-231 cells were procured from Korean cell line bank, Seoul. The cell counting kit-8 (CCK-8) cytotoxicity assay was performed on MDA-MB-231 cells with various concentrations of N-CDs. The MDA-MB-231 cells were seeded in a 96 well plate at a density of 5×103 cells per well. The synthesized N-CDs at various concentrations ranging from 0 to 20μgmL−1 (0, 5, 10, 15 and 20μgmL−1) were added into the wells and incubated for 24h (medium contains 10% of fetal bovine serum and 1% of penicillin-streptomycin mixture). The cells were grown in a CO2 incubator at 37°C and 5% CO2. After 24h, 10μgmL−1 of a CCK-8 solution (Dojindo laboratories, Kumamoto, Japan) was added to each well and incubated for 4h. After 4h, the absorbance was measured at 450nm using a microplate reader (Bio-rad). The cell viability was calculated according to the following Eq. (2).
(2)
Cell viability
( % ) =
Abs
( S )
-
Abs
( B )
Abs
( C )
-
Abs
( B )
× 100
where Abs (
S
), Abs (
B
) and Abs (
C
) are the absorbance of sample, blank and control, respectively.

The MDA-MB231 cells were cultured with 10μgmL−1 of N-CDs and without N-CDs in a growth medium supplemented with 10% of fetal bovine serum and 1% of penicillin-streptomycin mixture. The cells were grown in a CO2 incubator at 37°C and 5% CO2 for 4h. After incubation the medium was removed and the cells were washed with phosphate buffer solution (PBS). The N-CDs incubated cells were analyzed using a Nikon eclipse Ti fluorescence microscopy under ultraviolet excitation.

The electrocatalytic activity of synthesized N-CDS was tested towards O2 saturated 0.1M aqueous KOH (ORR) by rotating disk electrode method at a scan rate of 10mVs−1 using a EC-Lab Bio-logic instrument. Three electrode systems were used for this study. In this system, glassy carbon (3mm diameter) electrode was used as a working electrode, a platinum wire and saturated calomel electrode (SCE) served as the counter and reference electrodes, respectively. The active surface of GC is around 0.071cm2. To prepare the N-CDs modified GC electrode, the catalyst ink was prepared as follows, a 2.5mg of N-CDs, 80μL of IPA, 15μL of DI water and 5μL of Nafion solution was mixed and ultrasonicated for 30min in order to make fine dispersion (slurry). A drop of ink was then coated on the active surface of a GC and allowed it to dry for 6h at room temperature. After drying the GC electrode, it was weighted and subtracted from the blank GC electrode to get weight of the active materials on the active GC. The weight of active compound was around 1mg.

The morphology and size of the N-CDs were examined by TEM. Fig. 2 (a) shows that the resulting N-CDs were monodispersed without apparent aggregation. The HRTEM image (inset of Fig. 2(a)) display a lattice spacing (d-spacing value/interlayer distance) of 0.21nm, which correspond to the C(100) facet of graphite [33,34]. The ordered lattice fringes confirmed that the synthesized N-CDs were ordered. The size distribution histogram (Fig. 2(b)) shows that the N-CDs had a narrow size distribution. The resulting N-CDs are mainly distributed in the ranges from 5 to 10nm with an mean size of 8nm and possess a nearly spherical shape. Fig.2(c) shows the high angle annular dark field (HAADF) scanning transmission electron microscopy (STEM) image of resulting N-CDs. The corresponding elemental maps displayed in Fig. 2(d)–(f) illustrates the wide distribution of C and the sparse presence of N and O in comparison, there by qualitatively confirming the compositional data through XPS analysis. EDAX (Fig. 2(g)) analysis reveals the presence of C, N, and O elements. In Addition, Cu signals were also observed by EDAX due to the copper TEM grids. In consistent with HRTEM analysis, the AFM topography image (Fig. 2(h)) of the resulted N-CDs shows the monodisperse CDs with an mean size of 9nm [35].

The phase, structure and degree of crystallinity of the synthesized N-CDs was determined by XRD. Fig. 3 (a) shows a typical wide-angle XRD pattern of the N-CDs. The pattern clearly showed an intense diffraction peak at approximately 24.5° 2θ, which was indexed to the C(002) plane and the weak XRD peaks at approximately 43 and 54° 2θ were assigned to the C(100) and C(004) plane, respectively. In general, graphitic carbon shows a XRD peak at 26° (2θ) for C(002) plane but in the case of our N-CDs appeared at 24.5° might due to the little amorphous nature and increasing the interlayer distance in CDs. The outer surface of the CDs posses little amorphous nature due functionalization of nitrogen and other moiety present from the peach extract which was confirmed by FTIR spectroscopy analysis. The entire XRD patterns revealed the moderate graphitic structure of synthesized N-CDs [36–38]. The d-spacing value (spacing between the layers of atoms) was calculated using the Bragg Eq. (3) based on the position of the C(002) and C(100) reflection peaks. The calculated interlayer distance was approximately 0.36 and 0.21nm for the reflection peak of C(002) and C(100), respectively. The calculated interlayer distance matched well with the results obtained by HRTEM.
(3)
n λ = 2 dsin
θ
( or )
d =
n λ
2 sin
θ
where n is a positive integer (1), λ is the wavelength of incident X-ray (λ=1.54Å) and θ is the angle between the incident rays and the surface of the materials.

Raman spectroscopy is one of the most powerful non-invasive tools for the characterization of carbon nanostructured materials. The Raman spectrum of the synthesized N-CDs was acquired, which revealed two distinct peaks at 1340 and 1590cm−1 dubbed as D and G-bands, respectively (Fig. 3(b)). The D-band is related to defects in the graphitic sheets and the G-band is related to the vibration of the sp2-hybridized carbon atoms in the graphitic sheets [39,40]. The peak intensities of the D-band and G-band are denoted as ID and IG , respectively. The graphitization of carbon nanostructured materials was determined by ID /IG values [41,42]. The ID /IG values were around 0.75 and the low intensity ratio of the D to G band (ID
< IG ) indicates that the CDs obtained had a moderate graphitic structure. The data shows that the N-CDs had been synthesized without major carbonaceous impurities.

FT-IR spectroscopy was employed to investigate the N-CDs surface and functional groups. Fig. 3(c) and (d) shows the FT-IR spectra of the extract of unripe peach and resulting N-CDs, respectively. The unripe peach exhibited a characteristic intense broad absorption bands around at approximately 3250cm−1, corresponding to the OH and NH stretching vibrations of amine groups [43,44]. The weak absorption bands at 2855 and 2925cm−1 were assigned to the CH symmetric and asymmetric stretching vibrations respectively. The signal of the aromatic CC stretching at 1645cm−1 indicates the presence of a sp2 hybridized honeycomb lattice. The characteristic CN stretching vibrations at 1445 cm−1 can be observed in spectrum, which is consistent with XPS analysis of N-CDs (see Fig. 4 ). The bending vibration of the CO bond and stretching peak of the COC bond appear at 1400/1040 and 1300cm−1, respectively. The peaks at 1240cm−1 indicate the presence of epoxy groups for typical graphitic carbon. The peach extract and the synthesized N-CDs had similar functional groups according to the FT-IR spectra. The intensity of the CH2 functional group decreased and the CC functional group increased for the synthesized N-CDs compared to the peach extract. This might be due to the formation of CDs during the hydrothermal process. These results are consistent with the FT-IR measurements of N-CDs reported elsewhere [45]. Inset Fig. 3(d) shows the proposed structure of synthesized N-CDs with functional groups. The N-CDs possess graphitic in nature but surface was amorphous and functionalized and the resulting passivated N-CDs. Hence, the high graphitization turns to moderate graphitization which was confirmed by XRD. The plausible formation mechanism of N-CDs was involves dehydration, polymerization, carbonization/aromatization, and passivation as per earlier report [46].

Fig. 4 shows the XPS scans of the carbon level (C1s), nitrogen level (N1s), and oxygen level (O1s). In the survey scan (Fig. 4(a)), three distinct peaks were observed at 284.6, 399.6 and 531.6eV, which were assigned to the 1s orbitals of the carbon atoms, nitrogen and oxygen, respectively. The XPS of the C1s level (Fig. 4(b)) revealed the presence of three peaks at 284.45, 285.60, and 286.68eV which were assigned to the CC (sp2)/CC (sp3), CN/CN and CO/CO bonds, respectively [47–49]. The XPS fitting of N1s level (Fig.4(c)) showed a single peak at 399.5eV, revealing the presence of a CN group on the surface of CDs [50,51]. In addition, XPS fitting of O1s showed two peaks at 530.65 and 532.3eV associated oxygen in the CO and COH/COC in the CDs [52–54]. The atomic percentage of carbon, nitrogen and oxygen is approximately 76.59, 8.77 and 14.64%, respectively for synthesized N-CDs which was obtained from XPS results. Moreover, XPS strongly suggested that the synthesized CDs contain oxygen and nitrogen.

The optical properties of the N-CDs were examined using UV–Vis and fluorescence spectroscopy [55]. Fig. 5 shows the UV–Vis spectrum and the corresponding fluorescence spectra of the resulting N-CDs in water. The UV–Vis spectra (Fig. 5(a)) showed the N-CDs absorbed peak at around 270nm and the shoulder peak around 310nm, which were assigned to the π-π∗ and n-π∗ transitions of the carbonyl group (CC/CO) or amine moiety present over the N-CDs, respectively [56,57]. Fig. 5(b) shows the excitation-dependent fluorescence emission spectra of the synthesized N-CDs. The synthesized N-CDs emitted fluorescence at 400nm with an excitation wavelength of 310nm. Fig. 5(b) reveals the red shift of N-CD while increasing the excitation wavelength. The intensity of fluorescence gradually increases with increasing excitation wavelength (310–325nm) then decreased (330–350nm). The synthesized N-CDs emitted a maximum fluorescence wavelength is 400, 401, 402, 404, 407, 409,415, 419 and 426nm with an excitation wavelength of 310, 315, 320, 325, 330, 335, 340, 345 and 350nm, respectively. The maximum fluorescence intensity was observed with an excitation wavelength of 325nm. The QY of the synthesized N-CDs was calculated by measuring the fluorescence intensity in an aqueous dispersion by using the Eq. (1). The QY of N-CDs was measured at an excitation wavelength of 325nm, and the yield was around 15%. This suggests that the synthesized N-CDs possess a high fluorescence quantum yield. Table 1 shows the comparison of the optical characteristics, quantum yield, size and applications of the synthesized N-CDs derived from various eco-friendly carbon sources such as fruit extract and chemical compounds. The CDs synthesized using extract of unripe peach produced high florescence quantum yield with excellent application then compared to the reported quantum yield and applications [6–10,12–14,19].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrocatalytic oxygen reduction reaction of N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: N-CDs, N-CNTs and N-graphene were reported to show better electrocatalytic activity then the commercially available noble metal (Pt/C) based catalysts towards ORR and other reactions [58–62]. In addition to the fluorescent properties, N-CDs are also expected to be good electrocatalysts for the ORR. The electrocatalytic efficacy of the synthesized N-CDs towards the ORR was explored using cyclic voltammetry (CV) and linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) by rotating disk electrode (RDE) method.

In this study, the electrocatalytic activity of the N-CDs modified glassy carbon (GC) in a 0.1M KOH electrolyte (N2 and O2 gas saturated) were analyzed. Fig. 6 (a) shows the typical CV curves of ORR in N2 and O2 saturated 0.1M KOH electrolyte at the scan rate of 10mVs−1. A large cathodic peak at 0.72V was observed in O2 saturated and not in N2 saturated using 0.1M KOH electrolyte. The onset potential of the N-CDs was 0.94V thus the synthesized N-CDs shows excellent electrocatalytic performance in 0.1M KOH solution. Fig. 6(b), presents the LSV of the N-CDs modified GC electrode in N2 and O2 saturated 0.1M aqueous KOH solution at a rotation speed of 1600 RPM. In N2 saturated KOH, there is no obvious reduction peak observed between the scanned potential range, 1–0.14V Vs reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE), whereas in the O2 saturated KOH solution, a clear O2 reduction peak was observed at 0.45V Vs RHE, and the onset potential of ORR at N-CDs commences around 0.94V, which suggested that the synthesized N-CDs show electrocatalytic activity towards the reduction of O2 [63,64]. In addition, to determine the kinetics of ORR by N-CDs, the LSV experiment was carried out at different rotation speeds. Fig. 6(c), shows the LSV of the N-CDs modified GC at various rotation speeds. The results suggested that, with increasing rotation speed of the RDE, the limiting current density also improved, suggesting rapid electron transfer at higher rotation speeds. From the results, the Koutecky–Levich (K–L) plot was derived, as shown in Fig. 6(d). The K–L Eq. (4) can be represented as follows:
(4)
1
j
lim
=
1
j
k
+
1
( 0.62
nFAD
2 / 3
ω
1 / 2
υ
- 1 / 6
C )
where n is the number of electrons involved in the O2 reduction, F is Faraday’s Constant (96485.5C/mol), A is the electrode area (in cm2), D is the diffusion coefficient of saturated O2 (1.9×10−5 cm2/s), ω is the rotation rate of the electrode in radians per second (2πrpm/60), υ is the kinematic viscosity of the electrolyte (0.01009cm2/s) and C is the bulk O2 concentration (1.2×10−6 mol/cm3). From the above equation, a plot of 1/ω1/2 vs. 1/jlim will give a straight line of K–L plot, from the intercept value the number of electrons involved can be calculated.

The K-L plot for the N-CDs at four different potential such as 0.15, 0.20, 0.25, and 0.30V, was derived and the n values calculated at these potential were 1.90, 1.85, 1.80 and 1.82, respectively, indicating that O2 reduction follows mainly a 2 electron pathway by reducing to hydrogen peroxide [65]. The catalytic activity of N-CDs might be due to the surface defects of N-CDs, and the uneven charge distribution from electron donation and the back donation induced by heteroatoms (N) processes facilitate O2 dissociation and reduction [66]. The reduction pathways of 2 and 4 electrons have unique significance, depending on the applications. In the fuel cell processes, the 4-electron direct pathway is highly preferred whereas, the 2-electron reduction pathway is used in industry for H2O2 production.

This study investigated their performance as fluorescent cell labels by incubating MDA-MB-231 cells with the N-CDs (10μgmL−1) for 4h. After incubation, the cells were washed and their optical properties were examined by fluorescence microscopy. Prior to considering the utility of the N-CDs as fluorescent probes for cell imaging, it is fascinating to explore their toxicity towards MDA-MB-231 cells. Therefore, the cytotoxicity of the N-CDs was further tested by a CCK-8 assay. The MDA-MB-231 cell viability was approximately 100% at a N-CDs concentration of 10μgmL−1; however, the cell viability decreased slightly from 100% to 99% when the N-CDs concentration was increased from 10 to 20μgmL−1, which indicates that the CDs did not induce the death of MDA-MB-231 cell (Fig. 7 (a)). The N-CDs did not exhibit any cytotoxicity towards the cells and were tolerable, even at high concentrations, which confirms that the CDs are cytocompatible and do not induce cell death [67,68]. Based on the good aqueous monodispersity, high fluorescence quantum yield and low cytotoxicity, the resulting N-CDs were used as fluorescence imaging probes. As shown in Fig. 7(b) and (c), N-CDs incubated MDA-MB-231 cells became brightly illuminated under the microscope. Fig.7(e), MDA-MB-231 cells incubated without N-CDs shows the absence of fluorescence emission. These results show that the N-CDs can be taken up rapidly (large amount of N-CDs was internalized) by the cells and were used successfully as probes for the bioimaging of cells without any cytotoxicity [46].

N-CDs were synthesized by a single step hydrothermal-carbonization process using the extract of unripe peach and aqueous ammonia as the carbon precursor and nitrogen dopant, respectively. The surface functionalization and chemical compositions of the N-CDs were revealed by FT-IR spectroscopy and XPS analysis. The mean size of the N-CDs was approximately 8nm with an interlayer distance of 0.21nm and good quantum yield (15%). All the structural characterizations strongly showed that the synthesized N-CDs possess a good degree of graphitization with excellent nitrogen doping. The synthesized N-CDs possess excellent dispersity in DI water, smaller size, bright fluorescence emission with a good quantum yield and low cytotoxicity, as examined by a CKK-8 assay. The N-CDs were effectively internalized into the MDA-MB-231 cells and showed strong fluorescence of N-CDs within the cells. The cytotoxicity assays showed that the resulting N-CDs are highly biocompatible. Therefore, the reported N-CDs can be employed in biomedical research, such as bioimaging and drug delivery in nanobiotechnology. In addition to the biomedical applications, the synthesized N-CDs exhibits a good catalytic activity in an alkaline medium (0.1M KOH) with remarkable ORR of about 0.72V vs RHE and O2 reduction follows mainly 2 electron pathway by reducing to H2O2.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the Nano Material Technology Development Program of the Korean National Research Foundation (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Education, Science and Technology (2012M3A7B4049675). This work was also supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) grant funded by the Korea government (MSIP) (NRF-2014R1A2A1A11052391) and Priority Research Centers Program (2014R1A6A1031189).
